summary_text:
S:
The patient is a 22-month-old female with Smith-Magenis syndrome, born prematurely at 34 and 3 weeks. She experienced feeding difficulties as an infant, including episodes of apnea during feeding, and was initially treated with cow's milk, subsequently switched to a rice protein formula. She has been evaluated by a variety of specialists, including a neurologist, allergist, gastroenterologist, ophthalmologist, and audiologist. The patient reports frequent urination and increased fluid intake. She has had multiple therapies, including feeding therapy, occupational therapy, and physical therapy. She is currently undergoing speech therapy and will begin within the next couple of weeks. The patient's mother reports that she has had many therapies and has seen many specialists. The patient's twin sister is doing well and is of similar size and height. The patient is fussy when examined. The patient's reflexes are mild hypotonia of the lower extremities in her arms. The patient's current medications include rice protein formula and water. The patient's family lives in Dallas, Georgia. The patient has no family history of kidney failure, dialysis, or transplant.

O:
Blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg. Heart rate is 130 bpm. Temperature is 98.6°F. Lung sounds are clear to auscultation bilaterally. Heart sounds are regular. Neurological exam reveals mild hypotonia of the lower extremities. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation. Extraocular movements are full. The patient's ears are unremarkable. The patient's skin is warm and dry. Physical exam reveals a well-nourished female, who is slightly fussy when examined. The patient's eyes are small appearing.

A:
Katherine is a 22-month-old female with Smith-Magenis syndrome, presenting with feeding difficulties and frequent urination. The patient's symptoms are consistent with congenital anomalies of the kidney and urinary tract, as reported in the literature. Further investigation with a kidney ultrasound is warranted to assess for abnormalities.

P:
Order a kidney ultrasound to evaluate for congenital anomalies of the kidney and urinary tract. Schedule follow-up at the town center location if abnormalities are identified on the ultrasound. Arrange for a consultation with a pediatric nephrologist if indicated. Counsel the patient's parents on the importance of adherence to the prescribed feeding regimen and fluid intake. Schedule a follow-up appointment in 4 weeks to assess progress and address any concerns.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Renal screening tests.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Katherine Lopez is a 22-month-old female, born at 34 weeks +3 days gestation, diagnosed with the rare Smith-Magenis syndrome and associated global developmental delays. She was referred to nephrology by genetics who recommended renal screening tests. At birth, the patient weighed 4 pounds 8 ounces, exhibited difficulties during eating where she would stop breathing, and challenges regulating her body temperature; determined to be typical issues associated with premature birth. The patient has seen many specialists, including GI for severe reflux, a dietitian who recommended discontinuing consumption of cow’s milk, and a neurologist who diagnosed her with Smith-Magenis syndrome. Additionally, she has been seen by a geneticist, orthopedist, ophthalmologist, and audiologist. The patient is not ambulatory, did not sit up unassisted until 1 years old, and is currently crawling and attempting to take steps. She will be starting speech therapy in the next few weeks, and currently she is in occupational, feeding, and physical therapy. The geneticist informed the patient's mother that patients with Smith-Magenis syndrome usually have kidney issues where “the kidneys fuse into one” and issues with their legs.

The patient has approximately 8 to 10 wet diapers in a 24-hour period. Her mother notes that she is doing better with drinking fluids and she urinates “a lot”. The patient drinks Ripple Pea Protein milk instead of cow’s milk, water and water mixed with a small amount of juice. Her twin sister is approximately the same size, and their weight is essentially the same, she is healthy, and the mom denies any known health concerns or diagnoses. The mom states that Katherine is shorter and seems chubbier related to her syndrome. She also has a 9-year-old brother who is diagnosed with autism.

BIRTH HISTORY

Twin gestation. Birth weight 4 pounds 8 ounces.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Smith-Magenis syndrome.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient accompanied to appointment by her mother.
Sibling: 9-year-old brother and twin sister.

FAMILY HISTORY

Brother: 9 years old, positive for autism.
Twin sister: absence of Smith-Magenis syndrome, no known health conditions.
No known family history of genetic conditions, kidney failure, dialysis, or kidney transplant.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Constitutional
Well-nourished female, slightly fussy when examined.

Eyes
Presences of microphthalmia.

Genitourinary
Normal external female genitalia.

Musculoskeletal
Extremities: Presence of mild hypotonia of lower extremities and the arms.

ASSESSMENT

• Smith-Magenis syndrome.
Katherine is a 22-month-old former 34+3-week-old twin with Smith-Magenis syndrome.
Several organ systems can be affected by this chromosomal deletion syndrome and congenital anomalies of the kidney and urinary tract have been reported in the literature.

PLAN

We will obtain a kidney ultrasound screening today. If there are abnormalities on the kidney ultrasound, we will determine the next steps and future follow-up. The family lives in Dallas, Georgia, so her follow-up should be at the Town Center location.

INSTRUCTIONS

Complete ultrasound today. I will call patient with results and possible next steps.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient is fussy when examined.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Being fussy when examined is an observed exam finding, not a patient-reported symptom or history.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's reflexes are mild hypotonia of the lower extremities in her arms.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Description of reflexes and hypotonia is part of the physical/neurologic exam, which belongs in Objective, not Subjective.

section_miscategorizations.text: She has been evaluated by a variety of specialists, including a neurologist, allergist, gastroenterologist, ophthalmologist, and audiologist.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Prior specialist evaluations are part of documented medical history and prior workup; they are more appropriately organized under Objective, though inclusion in Subjective is not highly misleading.

section_miscategorizations.text: She has had multiple therapies, including feeding therapy, occupational therapy, and physical therapy. She is currently undergoing speech therapy and will begin within the next couple of weeks.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Completed and ongoing formal therapies are elements of prior/ongoing treatment rather than current subjective symptoms; they fit better under Objective history of care, though their placement in Subjective is not critically misleading.
